notes: Single-pass review, thorough but no iteration
Correctly identified all 3 content-level issues
Failed all 6 process/protocol expectations

Baselines consistently pass content expectations (identifying domain-specific issues like SQL injection, SPOF, missing auth) but fail all process expectations (iterative rounds, weighted scoring, stagnation detection, artifact revision). This confirms the skill adds structured iterative refinement, not domain knowledge.
With-skill runs achieve 100% pass rate across all 3 evals with zero variance, demonstrating reliable protocol adherence regardless of artifact type (design doc, implementation plan, or code).

Without-skill pass rates cluster tightly at 0.33-0.44. The flawed-design baseline scores slightly higher (0.44) because it produced themed review sections that satisfied the 'quality gate announcement' expectation, while the other two baselines did not.

The skill roughly doubles execution time (154.6s vs 64.2s mean) and token usage (21.2K vs 10.8K mean). This overhead reflects the iterative revision cycles -- each with_skill run performed 3-4 rounds of attack-revise-recheck.

All with_skill runs drove weighted scores to zero (no Fatal or Significant issues remaining). Score progressions: flawed-design 13->3->1->0, weak-plan 13->5->2->0, vulnerable-code 12->3->0. Each showed strictly decreasing scores with no stagnation.

The 6 process expectations (iterative rounds, severity labels, weighted scoring, revision, stagnation detection, gate announcement) are the primary discriminators between skill and baseline runs. The 3 content expectations pass in both configurations, confirming they test domain knowledge rather than skill behavior.
